---
title: "Results"
---



## 3. Results

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

from pathlib import Path
import os
import requests
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, root_mean_squared_error, r2_score
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV, TimeSeriesSplit
from xgboost import XGBRegressor
import yfinance as yf
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()

START_DATE = "2015-01-01"
END_DATE = "2025-12-31"
API_KEY = os.getenv("FRED_API_KEY")

def fred_download(series_id, start_date=START_DATE, end_date=END_DATE):
    url = "https://api.stlouisfed.org/fred/series/observations"
    params = {
        "series_id": series_id,
        "api_key": API_KEY,
        "file_type": "json",
        "observation_start": start_date,
        "observation_end": end_date
    }
    r = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=30)
    r.raise_for_status()
    data = r.json()["observations"]
    df = pd.DataFrame(data)[["date", "value"]]
    df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])
    df["value"] = pd.to_numeric(df["value"], errors="coerce")
    return df

def yf_download(ticker, start_date=START_DATE, end_date=END_DATE):
    df = yf.download(ticker, start=start_date, end=end_date, auto_adjust=False, progress=False)
    if isinstance(df.columns, pd.MultiIndex):
        df.columns = df.columns.get_level_values(0)
    df = df.reset_index()[["Date", "Close"]]
    df["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["Date"])
    return df

def load_or_download_csv(path, loader):
    path = Path(path)
    if path.exists():
        return pd.read_csv(path)
    df = loader()
    df.to_csv(path, index=False)
    return df

def standardize_columns(df, date_col, value_col, new_value_name):
    out = df.copy()
    out[date_col] = pd.to_datetime(out[date_col])
    out[value_col] = pd.to_numeric(out[value_col], errors="coerce")
    out = out.rename(columns={date_col: "date", value_col: new_value_name})
    out = out[["date", new_value_name]].sort_values("date")
    return out
```

```{python}
#| label: load-data
#| include: false

sp500_raw = load_or_download_csv("data/sp500.csv", lambda: yf_download("^GSPC"))
vix_raw = load_or_download_csv("data/vix.csv", lambda: yf_download("^VIX"))
gold_raw = load_or_download_csv("data/gold.csv", lambda: yf_download("GC=F"))
dxy_raw = load_or_download_csv("data/dxy.csv", lambda: yf_download("DX-Y.NYB"))
rate_raw = load_or_download_csv("data/dgs10.csv", lambda: fred_download("DGS10"))
```

```{python}
#| label: standardize-data
#| include: false

sp500 = standardize_columns(sp500_raw, "Date", "Close", "sp500_close")
vix = standardize_columns(vix_raw, "Date", "Close", "vix")
gold = standardize_columns(gold_raw, "Date", "Close", "gold")
dxy = standardize_columns(dxy_raw, "Date", "Close", "dxy")
rate = standardize_columns(rate_raw, "date", "value", "rate")


display(sp500.shape)
```

```{python}
#| label: merge-data
#| include: false

df_merged = sp500.merge(vix, on="date", how="left")
df_merged = df_merged.merge(rate, on="date", how="left")
df_merged = df_merged.merge(gold, on="date", how="left")
df_merged = df_merged.merge(dxy, on="date", how="left")
df_merged = df_merged.sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True)
```

```{python}
#| label: missing-before
#| include: false

display(df_merged.isnull().sum())
display(df_merged.shape)
```

```{python}
#| label: cleaning-missing-values
#| include: false

df_filled = df_merged.copy()
df_filled["rate"] = df_filled["rate"].ffill()
df_clean = df_filled.dropna(subset=["gold", "dxy"]).sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True)
```

```{python}
#| label: missing-after
#| include: false

display(df_clean.isnull().sum())
display(df_clean.shape)
```

```{python}
#| label: construct-outcome
#| include: false

df_clean["return"] = df_clean["sp500_close"].pct_change()
df_clean["volatility"] = df_clean["return"].rolling(7).std()
df_clean["log_volatility"] = np.log(df_clean["volatility"])

analysis_df = df_clean.dropna(subset=["return", "volatility", "log_volatility"]).reset_index(drop=True)
analysis_df["weekday"] = analysis_df["date"].dt.day_name()
display(analysis_df.shape)
```

### Data Exploration

```{python}
#| label: fig-interactive-timeseries-plot
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Time series plots of the S&P 500, predictors and volatility"
#| fig-align: center

series_info = [
    ("sp500_close", "S&P 500 close Price"),
    ("vix", "Implied Volatility(VIX)"),
    ("rate", "10-year Treasury Yield (rate)"),
    ("gold", "Gold Price (gold)"),
    ("dxy", "U.S. Dollar Index (DXY)"),
    ("volatility", "7-day window realized volatility (volatility)"),
    ("log_volatility", "Log of volatility (log_volatility)")
]

fig = go.Figure()

for i, (col, title) in enumerate(series_info):
    hover_text = (
        "Date: " + analysis_df["date"].astype(str) + "<br>" +
        title + ": " + analysis_df[col].round(4).astype(str)
    )
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=analysis_df["date"],
            y=analysis_df[col],
            mode="lines",
            name=title,
            text=hover_text,
            hoverinfo="text",
            visible=(i == 0)
        )
    )

buttons = [
    dict(
        label=title,
        method="update",
        args=[
            {"visible": [j == i for j in range(len(series_info))]},
            {"title.text": title, "yaxis.title.text": title}
        ]
    )
    for i, (col, title) in enumerate(series_info)
]

fig.update_layout(
    updatemenus=[dict(
        active=0,
        buttons=buttons,
        x=0.0,
        xanchor="left",
        y=1.2,
        yanchor="top"
    )],
    title=series_info[0][1],
    xaxis_title="Date",
    yaxis_title=series_info[0][1],
    height=450,
    template="plotly_white",
    margin=dict(t=120)
)

fig.show()
```

This time series plot @fig-interactive-timeseries-plot shows that the S&P 500, all predictors, volatility and log-volatility over time. We can use the dropdown menu to switch between the plots. 

We can see that S&P 500 and Gold has similar patterns, 
VIX and 7-day window realized volatility has similar patterns, also 10-year Treasury Yield and U.S. Dollar Index(DXY) has similar patterns.

```{python}
#| label: fig-volatility-distribution
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of realized volatility before and after log transformation"
#| fig-align: center

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

axes[0].hist(analysis_df["volatility"], bins=40)
axes[0].set_title("Volatility")
axes[0].set_xlabel("volatility")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Count")

axes[1].hist(analysis_df["log_volatility"], bins=40)
axes[1].set_title("Log Volatility")
axes[1].set_xlabel("log(volatility)")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Count")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

This hitogram plots @fig-volatility-distribution show the distribution of volatility and log-volatility. The volatility is right-skewed, and log-volatility is more symmetric and hence better for modeling OLS linear regression.

```{python}
#| label: fig-interactive-scatter-predictors
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Scatter plots of each predictor vs. log-volatility"
#| fig-align: center

feature_cols_eda = ["vix", "rate", "gold", "dxy"]
feature_labels = ["Implied Volatility(VIX)", "10-year Treasury Yield(rate)", "Gold", "U.S. Dollar Index(DXY)"]

fig = go.Figure()

for i, (col, label) in enumerate(zip(feature_cols_eda, feature_labels)):
    x = analysis_df[col]
    y = analysis_df["log_volatility"]

    hover_text = (
        label + ": " + x.round(4).astype(str) + "<br>" +
        "log(volatility): " + y.round(4).astype(str)
    )
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=x, y=y,
        mode="markers",
        name=label,
        text=hover_text,
        hoverinfo="text",
        marker=dict(opacity=0.4, size=4),
        visible=(i == 0)
    ))

    # OLS trend line
    m, b = np.polyfit(x.dropna(), y[x.notna()], 1)
    x_line = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), 100)
    y_line = m * x_line + b
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=x_line, y=y_line,
        mode="lines",
        name=f"{label} trend",
        line=dict(color="red", width=2),
        hoverinfo="skip",
        visible=(i == 0)
    ))

n = len(feature_cols_eda)
buttons = [
    dict(
        label=label,
        method="update",
        args=[
            {"visible": [j // 2 == i for j in range(2 * n)]},
            {"title.text": f"{label} vs. Log Volatility",
             "xaxis.title.text": label}
        ]
    )
    for i, (col, label) in enumerate(zip(feature_cols_eda, feature_labels))
]

fig.update_layout(
    updatemenus=[dict(
        active=0,
        buttons=buttons,
        x=0.0,
        xanchor="left",
        y=1.2,
        yanchor="top"
    )],
    title=f"{feature_labels[0]} vs. Log Volatility",
    xaxis_title=feature_labels[0],
    yaxis_title="log(volatility)",
    height=450,
    showlegend=False,
    template="plotly_white",
    margin=dict(t=120)
)

fig.show()
```

This scatter plot @fig-interactive-scatter-predictors shows the relationship between each predictor and log-volatility. 

VIX and log-volatility seem to have a positive relationship, most of the points are centered around the trend line. The Gold and rate do not have a clear relationship with log-volatility, the points are scattered everywhere, no clear pattern or a steep trend line. The trend line of DXY is slightly positive, but the data points are not very concentrated around the trend line.

So VIX has a strong positive linear relationship with log-volatility, while the other predictors do not show a clear linear relationship with the outcome.

```{python}
#| label: fig-weekday-boxplot
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of log-volatility by weekday (Monday to Friday)"
#| fig-align: center

day_order = ["Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday", "Thursday", "Friday"]

fig = go.Figure()

for day in day_order:
    vals = analysis_df.loc[analysis_df["weekday"] == day, "log_volatility"]
    hover_text = (
        "Weekday: " + day + "<br>" +
        "log(volatility): " + vals.round(4).astype(str)
    )
    fig.add_trace(go.Box(
        y=vals,
        name=day,
        text=hover_text,
        hoverinfo="text",
        boxpoints="outliers"
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Log-Volatility by Weekday",
    xaxis_title="Weekday",
    yaxis_title="log(volatility)",
    height=450,
    showlegend=False,
    template="plotly_white"
)

fig.show()
```


The boxplot @fig-weekday-boxplot shows the distribution of log-volatility by weekday. There is no a significant differnce in log-volatility distributionacross weekdays, the means are all around -5, and spreads are also similar.

```{python}
#| label: fig-weekday-ttest
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Pairwise t-test p-values for log-volatility across weekdays. Darker cells indicate smaller p-values."
#| fig-align: center

n = len(day_order)
p_matrix = np.full((n, n), np.nan)

for i, d1 in enumerate(day_order):
    for j, d2 in enumerate(day_order):
        if i == j:
            p_matrix[i, j] = np.nan
        elif i < j:
            g1 = analysis_df.loc[analysis_df["weekday"] == d1, "log_volatility"]
            g2 = analysis_df.loc[analysis_df["weekday"] == d2, "log_volatility"]
            _, p = stats.ttest_ind(g1, g2)
            p_matrix[i, j] = round(p, 4)
            p_matrix[j, i] = round(p, 4)


hover_matrix = []
for i, d1 in enumerate(day_order):
    row = []
    for j, d2 in enumerate(day_order):
        if i == j:
            row.append(f"{d1}<br>(self-comparison)")
        else:
            p = p_matrix[i, j]
            sig = " significant" if p < 0.05 else "not significant"
            row.append(f"{d1} vs {d2}<br>p-value: {p:.4f}<br>{sig}")
    hover_matrix.append(row)

fig = go.Figure(go.Heatmap(
    z=p_matrix,
    x=day_order,
    y=day_order,
    text=hover_matrix,
    hoverinfo="text",
    colorscale="RdYlGn",
    reversescale=True,
    zmin=0, zmax=1,
    colorbar=dict(title="p-value")
))

for i in range(n):
    for j in range(n):
        if i != j:
            fig.add_annotation(
                x=day_order[j], y=day_order[i],
                text=f"{p_matrix[i, j]:.3f}",
                showarrow=False,
                font=dict(size=12, color="black")
            )

fig.update_layout(
    title="Pairwise t-test p-values: log-volatility across weekdays",
    xaxis_title="Weekday",
    yaxis_title="Weekday",
    height=420,
    template="plotly_white"
)

fig.show()
```

The pairwise t-ttest results @fig-weekday-ttest further confirms the observation from the boxplot. All the p-values are substantially greater than 0.05, so we cannot say there are significant differences in log-volatility across weekdays.

```{python}
#| label: fig-correlation-heatmap
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Correlation heatmap for the outcome and explanatory variables"
#| fig-align: center

corr_vars = ["log_volatility", "vix", "rate", "gold", "dxy"]
corr_table = analysis_df[corr_vars].corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 6))
plt.imshow(corr_table, aspect="auto", vmin=-1, vmax=1)
plt.xticks(range(len(corr_table.columns)), corr_table.columns, rotation=45, ha="right")
plt.yticks(range(len(corr_table.index)), corr_table.index)
for i in range(len(corr_table.index)):
    for j in range(len(corr_table.columns)):
        plt.text(j, i, f"{corr_table.iloc[i, j]:.2f}", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9)
plt.colorbar()
plt.title("Correlation Matrix")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The correlation heatmap @fig-correlation-heatmap shows the correlation between the numeric variables and log-volatility. It also confirms the observations from the scatter plots. VIX and log-volatility have a strong positive correlation (0.76), and dxy has a small positive correlation (0.19), while the other two predictors, rate and gold, do not show a significant correlation with log-volatility.

### Model Fitting

```{python}
#| label: model-data
#| include: false

base_feature_cols = ["vix", "rate", "gold", "dxy"]
target_col = "log_volatility"

model_df = analysis_df[["date"] + base_feature_cols + ["weekday", target_col]].copy()


weekday_dummies = pd.get_dummies(model_df["weekday"], prefix="weekday", drop_first=False)
weekday_dummies = weekday_dummies.drop(columns=["weekday_Monday"])
model_df = pd.concat([model_df, weekday_dummies], axis=1)

feature_cols = base_feature_cols + list(weekday_dummies.columns)

train_size = int(len(model_df) * 0.8)

train_df = model_df.iloc[:train_size].copy()
test_df = model_df.iloc[train_size:].copy()

X_train = train_df[feature_cols]
y_train = train_df[target_col]
X_test = test_df[feature_cols]
y_test = test_df[target_col]
```

```{python}
#| label: fit-ols
#| include: false

ols_model = LinearRegression()
ols_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

```{python}
#| label: fit-rf
#| include: false

rf_search = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=RandomForestRegressor(random_state=370, n_jobs=-1),
    param_grid={
        "n_estimators": [200, 400, 600],
        "max_depth": [5, 15, 30],
        "min_samples_split": [2, 5, 10]
    },
    scoring="neg_root_mean_squared_error",
    cv=TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5),
    n_jobs=1
)

rf_search.fit(X_train, y_train)
best_rf = rf_search.best_estimator_
```

```{python}
#| label: fit-xgb
#| include: false

xgb_search = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=XGBRegressor(objective="reg:squarederror", random_state=370, n_jobs=-1),
    param_grid={
        "n_estimators": [200, 400, 600],
        "max_depth": [5, 15, 30],
        "learning_rate": [0.01, 0.05, 0.1],
        "subsample": [0.5, 0.8, 1.0]
    },
    scoring="neg_root_mean_squared_error",
    cv=TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5),
    n_jobs=1
)

xgb_search.fit(X_train, y_train)
best_xgb = xgb_search.best_estimator_
```

```{python}
#| label: evaluation
#| include: false

def evaluate_model(name, fitted_model, X_test, y_test):
    pred = fitted_model.predict(X_test)
    return pd.DataFrame({
        "model": [name],
        "rmse": [root_mean_squared_error(y_test, pred)],
        "mae": [mean_absolute_error(y_test, pred)],
        "r2": [r2_score(y_test, pred)]
    })

results = pd.concat([
    evaluate_model("OLS", ols_model, X_test, y_test),
    evaluate_model("Random Forest", best_rf, X_test, y_test),
    evaluate_model("XGBoost", best_xgb, X_test, y_test)
], ignore_index=True)
```


We will fit three models: OLS, Random Forest and XGBoost.

By grid searching the hyperparameters, we found the best hyperparameters for each model as follows @tbl-hyperparameter-table:
```{python}
#| label: tbl-hyperparameter-table
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Hyperparameters for each model"
#| tbl-align: center

hyperparameter_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Model": ["OLS", "Random Forest", "XGBoost"],
    "n_estimators": ["—", rf_search.best_params_["n_estimators"], xgb_search.best_params_["n_estimators"]],
    "max_depth": ["—", rf_search.best_params_["max_depth"], xgb_search.best_params_["max_depth"]],
    "min_samples_split": ["—", rf_search.best_params_["min_samples_split"], "—"],
    "learning_rate": ["—", "—", xgb_search.best_params_["learning_rate"]],
    "subsample": ["—", "—", xgb_search.best_params_["subsample"]]
})

display(hyperparameter_summary)
```

The model performance with the best hyperparameters @tbl-model-performance on test set is as follows:

```{python}
#| label: tbl-model-performance
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Model performance on test set"
#| tbl-align: center

results_table = results.sort_values("rmse").copy()
results_table["rmse"] = results_table["rmse"].round(3)
results_table["mae"] = results_table["mae"].round(3)
results_table["r2"] = results_table["r2"].round(3)
results_table = results_table.rename(columns={
    "model": "Model",
    "rmse": "RMSE",
    "mae": "MAE",
    "r2": "R2"
}).reset_index(drop=True)

display(results_table)
```

The table @tbl-model-performance shows that OLS has the smaller RMSE(0.369) and MAE(0.298), and higher R2(0.434) compared to other two non-linear models. This sugguests that the relationship between the predictors and log-volatility is largely linear, and there is limited nonlinear structure to exploit in the data set, so more flexible,non-linear models do not provide much improvement.

Comparing the XGBoost and Random Forest models, the XGBoost model has a slightly lower RMSE(0.384) and MAE(0.309), and higher R2(0.388) and hence a better performance than the Random Forest model. 

Worth noting, the R2 for all three models are relatively low, indicating that a substantial portion of volatility variation remains unexplained by the predictors due to the complexity of financial market and the limited predictors we used. 


```{python}
#| label: tbl-ols-coefficients
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Summary for OLS model"
#| tbl-align: center

X_arr = X_train.values.astype(float)   # ensure float64; bool dummy cols cause dtype('O') otherwise
y_arr = y_train.values.astype(float)
n_obs, n_feat = X_arr.shape

# Use coefficients directly from the fitted model
beta = np.concatenate([[ols_model.intercept_], ols_model.coef_])

# Compute SEs from residuals of the fitted model
X_aug = np.column_stack([np.ones(n_obs), X_arr])
residuals = y_arr - ols_model.predict(X_train)
sigma2 = np.sum(residuals ** 2) / (n_obs - n_feat - 1)
se_all = np.sqrt(sigma2 * np.diag(np.linalg.inv(X_aug.T @ X_aug)))

t_stats = beta / se_all
p_values = 2 * stats.t.sf(np.abs(t_stats), df=n_obs - n_feat - 1)

coef_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Feature": ["(Intercept)"] + feature_cols,
    "Coefficient": beta.round(4),
    "Std. Error": se_all.round(4),
    "t-statistic": t_stats.round(4),
    "p-value": p_values.round(4),
    "Sig.": ["***" if p < 0.001 else "**" if p < 0.01 else "*" if p < 0.05 else "" for p in p_values]
}).reset_index(drop=True)

display(coef_df)
```

The summary of the OLS model @tbl-ols-coefficients shows that VIX has the strongest positive association with log-volatility, since its t-statistic is the largest(50.57). Beside that, rate and gold are also statistically significant in 0.05 level, rate has a positive relationship and gold has a negative relationship with log-volatility. Other predictors, dxy and weekday dummies, are not significant, so the US dollar index has a limited explanatory power for log-volatility and there is no enough evidence of day-of-week effects in log-volatility.


```{python}
#| label: fig-feature-importance
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Feature importances for Random Forest and XGBoost"
#| fig-align: center

rf_importance = pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": feature_cols,
    "importance": best_rf.feature_importances_
}).sort_values("importance", ascending=True)

xgb_importance = pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": feature_cols,
    "importance": best_xgb.feature_importances_
}).sort_values("importance", ascending=True)

rf_hover = (
    "Feature: " + rf_importance["feature"] + "<br>" +
    "Importance: " + rf_importance["importance"].round(4).astype(str)
)
xgb_hover = (
    "Feature: " + xgb_importance["feature"] + "<br>" +
    "Importance: " + xgb_importance["importance"].round(4).astype(str)
)

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=["Random Forest", "XGBoost"],
    shared_xaxes=False
)

fig.add_trace(
    go.Bar(
        x=rf_importance["importance"],
        y=rf_importance["feature"],
        orientation="h",
        text=rf_hover,
        hoverinfo="text",
        marker_color="steelblue",
        name="Random Forest"
    ),
    row=1, col=1
)

fig.add_trace(
    go.Bar(
        x=xgb_importance["importance"],
        y=xgb_importance["feature"],
        orientation="h",
        text=xgb_hover,
        hoverinfo="text",
        marker_color="darkorange",
        name="XGBoost"
    ),
    row=1, col=2
)

fig.update_layout(
    title="Feature Importances: Random Forest vs. XGBoost",
    height=450,
    showlegend=False,
    template="plotly_white"
)
fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Importance", row=1, col=1)
fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Importance", row=1, col=2)

fig.show()
```

The feature importances ranking for both Random Forest and XGBoost models @fig-feature-importance are same. VIX is the dominant predictor for both models, followed by rate, dxy, and gold, and the weekday dummies are less important.

Furthermore, the Random Forest model put more weight on vix, the importance reaches 0.8794, while the XGBoost model put more weight on rate, the importance reaches 0.3222. This suggests that the Random Forest model is more sensitive to the changes in vix, while the importance of VIX in XGBoost model is 0.6299, slightly lower than the Random Forest model and weighted more on rest of the predictors.
